topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The accusation landed in my lap like a cold, wet stone. I stared at the printed page, the highlighted sections mocking me with their perfect, borrowed syntax. My hands, usually steady enough to ace any exam, were trembling slightly as I traced the lines of the cited text. How could this be? I’d spent weeks wrestling with the concepts, synthesizing ideas in my own clumsy, often awkward way. Now, it felt like someone had stolen the very air I was breathing. I looked up at Professor Albright, his expression unreadable, and managed a small, tight nod, the effort of composure making my throat feel suddenly dry. Every word I spoke felt hollow, an echo of something I hadn't truly owned.

[story 2]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed, a monotonous soundtrack to my rising panic. I kept re-reading the essay, the familiar arguments suddenly shifting into a landscape of alien vocabulary. My stomach churned, a tight knot forming just beneath my ribs. I tried to access the memory of my research, the late nights fueled by instant coffee, but the connections felt frayed, disconnected. When the department head called my name, the sound was sharp, cutting through the quiet. I stood up too quickly, knocking my chair slightly askew, and my gaze darted around the room, searching for an exit that didn't exist. I just wanted to sink into the floor.

[story 3]

It started with a single email, a sterile notification that felt disproportionate to the task at hand. I sat at my desk, the glow of the monitor reflecting in my wide eyes. The evidence presented against me was meticulously organized, each citation a tiny, damning piece of proof. I picked up my pen, intending to write a defense, but the ink seemed to swim on the page. My breath hitched. I looked at the stack of notes I’d painstakingly compiled, the diagrams I’d drawn, and they seemed utterly meaningless now. A strange detachment washed over me; it was as if I was watching a performance of my own life from a distance. I just kept blinking, unable to form a coherent response.
